Based on the web search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Claude Code hooks:

## What Are Claude Code Hooks?

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Think of hooks like git hooks, but for your AI agent.

## How They Work

When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler. A hook starts with an event, can optionally be narrowed with a matcher, receives structured input from Claude Code, and then executes one or more handlers.

## Types of Hooks

Command hooks run a shell command, receiving JSON input on stdin and communicating results through exit codes and stdout. HTTP hooks send the event's JSON input as an HTTP POST request to a URL. MCP tool hooks call a tool on an already-connected MCP server. Prompt hooks send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation.

## Key Benefits

Hooks fix the problem of relying on probabilistic behavior by giving you hard, deterministic control over what happens at every stage of the agent's lifecycle. The critical difference from prompt-based rules is that hooks are guaranteed to execute. If you tell Claude Code in your CLAUDE.md not to modify .env files, it will probably listen. If you set up a PreToolUse hook that blocks writes to .env files, it will always block them.

## Common Use Cases

Every time Claude Code writes or edits a file, a PostToolUse hook can run your formatter automatically, eliminating manual formatting reviews. A Stop or Notification hook can fire a desktop notification when Claude finishes or needs input, so you stop checking back every thirty seconds.

Based on the search results, here's what Claude Code hooks are:

## Overview

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Think of hooks like git hooks, but for your AI agent.

## How They Work

Hooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session. When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler. A hook starts with an event, can optionally be narrowed with a matcher, receives structured input from Claude Code, and then executes one or more handlers.

## Types of Hooks

Command hooks run a shell command - your script receives the event's JSON input on stdin and communicates results back through exit codes and stdout. HTTP hooks send the event's JSON input as an HTTP POST request to a URL, with the endpoint communicating results back through the response body. Prompt hooks send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation, with the model returning a yes/no decision as JSON.

## Key Use Cases

Every time Claude Code writes or edits a file, a PostToolUse hook can run your formatter automatically - no more manually running prettier after every change. The Stop event fires whenever Claude finishes responding, and if your hook returns a blocking signal, Claude is forced to keep working. Combine this with a prompt hook to get an AI agent that verifies its own work before stopping.

The official documentation is available at code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks for reference on hook events, configuration schema, and input/output formats, with a quickstart guide at the same site for automating actions with hooks.

Based on the search results, here's what Claude Code hooks are:

## Overview

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Think of hooks like git hooks, but for your AI agent.

## How They Work

A hook starts with an event, can optionally be narrowed with a matcher, receives structured input from Claude Code, and then executes one or more handlers. Hooks are configured with three levels of nesting: choose a hook event to respond to, add a matcher group to filter when it fires, and define one or more hook handlers to run when matched.

## Hook Types

There are several hook types: command hooks that run a shell command, HTTP hooks that send the event's JSON input as an HTTP POST request, prompt hooks that send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation returning a yes/no decision, and agent hooks that spawn a subagent to verify conditions.

## Key Benefits

The critical difference from prompt-based rules is that hooks are guaranteed to execute—if you set up a PreToolUse hook that blocks writes to .env files, it will always block them. Hooks serve as the missing layer between prompts and production systems, where behaviour stops being "best effort" and starts becoming enforced.

## Common Use Cases

Every time Claude Code writes or edits a file, a PostToolUse hook can run your formatter automatically, eliminating formatting inconsistencies. You can set up a PreToolUse hook that inspects every bash command before it runs to prevent dangerous operations.

Based on the search results, here's an overview of Claude Code hooks:

## What Are Claude Code Hooks?

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. They serve as the missing layer between prompts and production systems, where behaviour stops being "best effort" and starts becoming enforced.

## How They Work

Hooks are configured in three levels: choose a hook event to respond to, add a matcher group to filter when it fires, and define one or more hook handlers to run when matched. When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler.

## Hook Types

Claude Code supports several handler types:
- Command hooks (type: "command"): run a shell command, where your script receives the event's JSON input on stdin and communicates results back through exit codes and stdout.
- HTTP hooks (type: "http"): send the event's JSON input as an HTTP POST request to a URL, with the endpoint communicating results back through the response body.
- Prompt hooks (type: "prompt"): send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation, with the model returning a yes/no decision as JSON.
- Agent hooks (type: "agent"): spawn a subagent that can use tools like Read, Grep, and Glob to verify conditions before returning a decision.

## Common Use Cases

Every time Claude Code writes or edits a file, a PostToolUse hook can run your formatter automatically, eliminating manually running tools like gofmt or prettier after every change. The Stop event fires whenever Claude finishes responding, and if your hook returns a blocking signal, Claude is forced to keep working; combined with a prompt hook, you get an AI agent that verifies its own work before stopping.

Based on the web search results, here's what Claude Code hooks are:

## Overview

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Hooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session.

## Purpose

Hooks serve as lifecycle control points for explicit moments where the system can enforce, validate, or react, and fill the gap designed to address relying on a probabilistic system to behave deterministically. Rather than hoping Claude Code follows your rules through prompts alone, hooks guarantee certain behaviors will occur.

## How They Work

A hook starts with an event, can optionally be narrowed with a matcher, receives structured input from Claude Code, and then executes one or more handlers.

## Types of Hooks

Command hooks (type: "command") run a shell command with the event's JSON input on stdin and communicate results back through exit codes and stdout. Additionally, HTTP hooks (type: "http") send the event's JSON input as an HTTP POST request to a URL, with results communicated through the response body. Prompt hooks (type: "prompt") send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation, and the model returns a yes/no decision as JSON.

## Common Use Cases

Every time Claude Code writes or edits a file, a PostToolUse hook can run your formatter automatically, eliminating manually running formatting tools after every change. PreToolUse hooks can inspect every bash command before it runs to prevent dangerous commands. The Stop event fires whenever Claude finishes responding, and if your hook returns a blocking signal, Claude is forced to keep working; combining this with a prompt hook enables an AI agent that verifies its own work before stopping.

Copy, paste, customize.","url":"https://github.com/karanb192/claude-code-hooks"}]

Based on the search results, here's what Claude Code hooks are:

## Overview

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Think of hooks like git hooks, but for your AI agent.

## How They Work

Hooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session. When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler. A hook starts with an event, can optionally be narrowed with a matcher, receives structured input from Claude Code, and then executes one or more handlers.

## Types of Hooks

Command hooks run a shell command with JSON input on stdin. HTTP hooks send the event's JSON input as an HTTP POST request to a URL. MCP tool hooks call a tool on an already-connected MCP server. Prompt hooks send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation.

## Key Benefits

The critical difference from prompt-based rules is that hooks are guaranteed to execute. If you set up a PreToolUse hook that blocks writes to .env files, it will always block them. For anyone working on production codebases or in regulated environments, that distinction between "probably" and "always" is everything.

## Common Use Cases

- **Auto-formatting**: Every time Claude Code writes or edits a file, a PostToolUse hook can run your formatter automatically.
- **Safety**: Rather than giving Claude Code blanket permission and hoping for the best, you set up a PreToolUse hook that inspects every bash command before it runs.
- **Notifications**: A Stop or Notification hook can fire a desktop notification when Claude finishes or needs input.
